section.title: The Nazirite Vow
section.sectionRationale: Between the case laws of chapter 5 and the leaders' offerings of chapter 7 sits one of the most personal passages in Numbers: the voluntary vow of the Nazirite, by which any Israelite, man or woman, could set themselves apart to the LORD for a season, followed immediately by the priestly blessing that closes the whole Sinai legislation with grace rather than law.
section.thesis: Ordinary Israelites could voluntarily consecrate themselves to the LORD through the Nazirite vow, abstaining from the vine, from cutting their hair, and from contact with the dead, and this whole unit culminates in Aaron's blessing, God's own gracious words placed on His people's lives, showing that consecration finds its true rest not in vows kept but in the blessing God freely gives.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the voluntary vow of separation and its restrictions; the provision for accidental defilement during the vow; the offerings and rites that complete the vow; and the Aaronic blessing that closes the section.

units.label: The vow of separation
units.note: abstaining from the vine, from cutting hair, and from the dead

verses.text: And the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and tell them that if a man or woman makes a special vow, the vow of a {{nazir|Nazirite}}, to separate himself to the LORD,
he is to abstain from wine and strong drink. He must not drink vinegar made from wine or strong drink, and he must not drink any grape juice or eat fresh grapes or raisins.
All the days of his separation, he is not to eat anything that comes from the grapevine, not even the seeds or skins.

For the entire period of his vow of separation, no razor shall pass over his head. He must be holy until the time of his separation to the LORD is complete; he must let the hair of his head grow long.
Throughout the days of his separation to the LORD, he must not go near a dead body.
Even if his father or mother or brother or sister should die, he is not to defile himself, because the crown of consecration to his God is upon his head.
Throughout the time of his separation, he is holy to the LORD.
If someone suddenly dies in his presence and defiles his consecrated head of hair, he must shave his head on the day of his cleansing, the seventh day.
On the eighth day he must bring two turtledoves or two young pigeons to the priest at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.

And the priest is to offer one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering to make atonement for him, because he has sinned by being in the presence of the dead body. On that day he must consecrate his head again.
He must rededicate his time of separation to the LORD and bring a year-old male lamb as a guilt offering. But the preceding days shall not be counted, because his separation was defiled.
Now this is the law of the Nazirite when his time of separation is complete: He must be brought to the entrance to the Tent of Meeting,

and he is to present an offering to the LORD of an unblemished year-old male lamb as a burnt offering, an unblemished year-old female lamb as a sin offering, and an unblemished ram as a peace offering,
together with their grain offerings and drink offerings, and a basket of unleavened cakes made from fine flour mixed with oil and unleavened wafers coated with oil.
The priest is to present all these before the LORD and make the sin offering and the burnt offering.
He shall also offer the ram as a peace offering to the LORD, along with the basket of unleavened bread. And the priest is to offer the accompanying grain offering and drink offering.
Then at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting, the Nazirite is to shave his consecrated head, take the hair, and put it on the fire under the peace offering.

And the priest is to take the boiled shoulder from the ram, one unleavened cake from the basket, and one unleavened wafer, and put them into the hands of the Nazirite who has just shaved the hair of his consecration.

The priest shall then wave them as a wave offering before the LORD. This is a holy portion for the priest, in addition to the breast of the wave offering and the thigh that was presented. After that, the Nazirite may drink wine.

This is the law of the Nazirite who vows his offering to the LORD for his separation, in addition to whatever else he can afford; he must fulfill whatever vow he makes, according to the law of his separation.”
Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Tell Aaron and his sons: This is how you are to {{bless|bless}} the Israelites. Say to them:
‘May the LORD bless you and keep you;
may the LORD cause His face to shine upon you and be gracious to you;
may the LORD lift up His countenance toward you and give you peace.’
So they shall put My name on the Israelites, and I will bless them.”

nazir.gloss: One separated or consecrated. From a root meaning to separate, nazir describes someone who has voluntarily set themselves apart to the LORD for a period, marked visibly by uncut hair, abstinence from the vine, and avoidance of the dead.

bless.gloss: To bless, to speak good and favor over someone. Used three times in the rising rhythm of the Aaronic blessing, barak here is not a wish but a divinely commanded pronouncement through which God Himself blesses His people, verse 27.

basic.context: Chapter 6 gives instructions for the Nazirite vow, a voluntary act of consecration open to any Israelite, man or woman, who wished to set themselves apart to the LORD for a period by abstaining from wine, from cutting their hair, and from contact with the dead. The chapter closes with the famous priestly blessing Aaron and his sons were to speak over Israel.
basic.hermeneutics: The Nazirite vow was a real, voluntary practice available to ordinary people, not a status reserved for priests or a mere symbol. The blessing that follows is not a magic formula but words God commands His priests to speak, promising that He Himself will bless and keep His people; both halves of the chapter show holiness and grace held together, consecration and benediction.
basic.originalLanguages: The word nazir comes from a root meaning to separate or consecrate, describing someone set apart to the LORD for a time by a voluntary vow. The blessing itself turns on barak, to bless, spoken three times in a rising rhythm of blessing, grace, and peace that has shaped Jewish and Christian worship for millennia.
basic.typology: The Nazirite's total consecration, especially the uncut hair as a visible sign of devotion, anticipates the entire, unbroken consecration of Christ; more directly, the priestly blessing itself anticipates the greater blessing God gives His people in Christ, in whom every spiritual blessing is found, Ephesians 1:3.
basic.christ: The Aaronic blessing asks that God's face shine upon His people and grant them peace, a request answered fully in Jesus Christ, in whom God's face is truly seen, 2 Corinthians 4:6, and through whom lasting peace with God is made, Romans 5:1. Christ is greater than any Nazirite, wholly consecrated to God, and He is the one through whom the blessing spoken here becomes final and unbreakable.
basic.god: God delights to bless His people; the blessing is not something Israel must extract from a reluctant deity but words He commands His priests to speak because He intends to bless them. Even the strict rules governing the vow, and the requirement to start over if defiled, show a God who takes devotion seriously while still providing a way to begin again.
basic.crossReferences: Judges 13:5 and 1 Samuel 1:11 describe Samson and Samuel as Nazirites. Ephesians 1:3 says God has blessed believers with every spiritual blessing in Christ. 2 Corinthians 4:6 speaks of the light of the knowledge of God's glory in the face of Christ, echoing the Aaronic blessing's request that God's face shine on His people.
basic.oneStory: God provides a way for ordinary people to consecrate themselves to Him and then, in the same breath, speaks blessing over the whole of His people, grace that does not depend on the vow being kept perfectly. That blessing, God's face shining and giving peace, finds its full and final fulfillment in Jesus Christ, in whom every spiritual blessing is ours.

context.literary: This chapter forms a bridge between the case laws of chapter 5 and the tribal offerings of chapter 7, and its concluding blessing functions almost like a doxology closing the entire block of Sinai legislation that began back in Exodus.
context.historical: The Nazirite vow was evidently practiced across Israel's history, not confined to this legislation; Samson and Samuel, and later, in Jewish tradition, others associated with lifelong or temporary vows, show its lasting place in Israelite piety.
context.canonical: The Aaronic blessing became one of the most repeated texts in Jewish and Christian liturgy, and the discovery of this very blessing inscribed on ancient silver scrolls, among the oldest surviving biblical texts, testifies to its ancient and continuous use.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To provide instructions for a voluntary act of personal consecration open to any Israelite, and to close this section of the law with the priestly blessing, showing that the ultimate word spoken over God's people is one of grace, not merely obligation.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The Nazirite vow describes an optional practice available in ancient Israel; it is not a prescription that believers today must take similar vows, though New Testament figures with Nazirite-like consecration show its recognized value. The Aaronic blessing, by contrast, is prescriptive: the priests are directly commanded to speak it, and it remains a model of blessing still used in worship today.

debates.question: Does the New Testament's freedom from ceremonial vows mean the Nazirite vow has nothing to teach Christians today?

views.label: The vow is entirely obsolete
views.case: Since believers are not under the ceremonial law and have no requirement to abstain from wine or hair-cutting as an act of consecration, some see the Nazirite vow as historically interesting but without ongoing application.

views.label: The vow's principle of visible, voluntary consecration still applies
views.case: Others note that Paul himself apparently took a vow resembling a Nazirite vow, Acts 18:18, and that the underlying principle, voluntarily setting something apart to mark devotion to God, remains a valid, though not required, spiritual practice.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage's center of gravity is not the specific abstentions but the blessing that follows, God's grace resting on His people regardless of what vows they have or have not kept.

study.originalLanguages: Nazir, from a root meaning to separate, names someone set apart to the LORD by voluntary vow, marked visibly by uncut hair. The blessing that closes the chapter is built on barak, to bless, repeated three times with rising fullness, blessing, grace, and peace, followed by the LORD placing His own name on His people so that He Himself will bless them.

typology.type: The Nazirite's total, visible consecration to the LORD
typology.fulfillment: Christ, wholly consecrated to the Father from His conception and throughout His life, the truest fulfillment of a life set apart to God.

typology.type: The priestly blessing invoking God's face and peace
typology.fulfillment: Every spiritual blessing given to believers in Christ, Ephesians 1:3, and the peace with God secured through Him, Romans 5:1.

study.christ: The Aaronic blessing asks that God's face shine upon His people and grant them peace, a request answered fully in Jesus Christ, in whom God's face is truly seen, 2 Corinthians 4:6, and through whom lasting peace with God is made, Romans 5:1. Christ is greater than any Nazirite, wholly consecrated to God, and He is the one through whom the blessing spoken here becomes final and unbreakable for all who trust Him.
study.god: God delights to bless His people; the blessing is not something Israel must extract from a reluctant deity but words He commands His priests to speak because He intends to bless them. Even the strict rules governing the vow, and the requirement to start over if defiled, show a God who takes devotion seriously while still providing a way to begin again.

crossReferences.note: Describes Samson as a Nazirite from birth, showing the vow's place in Israel's later history.
Records Hannah's vow dedicating Samuel as a Nazirite, another example of this consecration in Israel's story.
Declares that God has blessed believers with every spiritual blessing in Christ, the fullest expression of the blessing pronounced here.
Speaks of the light of the knowledge of God's glory in the face of Christ, echoing the Aaronic blessing's prayer that God's face shine on His people.

study.oneStory: God provides a way for ordinary people to consecrate themselves to Him and then, in the same breath, speaks blessing over the whole of His people, grace that does not depend on the vow being kept perfectly. That blessing, God's face shining and giving peace, finds its full and final fulfillment in Jesus Christ, in whom every spiritual blessing is ours.

sermon.title: Benediction
sermon.why: Preached on Numbers 6:22-27 as part of a series on the elements of corporate worship, Keller unpacks the Aaronic blessing line by line, showing how God's blessing, grace, and peace are ultimately given to His people through Christ.

quiz.q: What were the main restrictions of the Nazirite vow described in this chapter?

options.text: Abstaining from wine and anything from the grapevine, not cutting the hair, and not going near a dead body.
options.note: Verses 3 to 6 list these three restrictions exactly.

options.text: Fasting from all food for the entire length of the vow.
options.note: No general fast is described; the restrictions are specific to wine, hair, and the dead.

options.text: Living permanently outside the camp.
options.note: Nazirites remained within the community; no requirement to leave the camp is given.

options.text: No specific restrictions are mentioned.
options.note: Verses 3 to 6 detail the restrictions clearly.

quiz.q: What had to happen if a Nazirite was suddenly defiled by a death nearby?

options.text: They shaved their head on the seventh day, offered specific sacrifices, and started the days of separation over again.
options.note: Verses 9 to 12 describe this exact process.

options.text: The vow was permanently cancelled with no remedy.
options.note: A remedy and a restart process are given, not a permanent cancellation.

options.text: Nothing; accidental defilement did not affect the vow.
options.note: This contradicts the explicit instructions in verses 9 to 12.

options.text: The text gives no instructions for this situation.
options.note: Verses 9 to 12 address it directly.

quiz.q: What offerings did a Nazirite bring when the vow was completed?

options.text: A burnt offering, a sin offering, a peace offering, along with grain and drink offerings and a basket of unleavened bread.
options.note: Verses 13 to 15 list these offerings in full.

options.text: Only a single lamb, with nothing else required.
options.note: This understates the fuller set of offerings actually required.

options.text: A monetary payment to the priest instead of any sacrifice.
options.note: The text specifies animal and grain offerings, not a monetary substitute.

options.text: No offerings are mentioned at the completion of the vow.
options.note: Verses 13 to 15 detail the required offerings.

quiz.q: What is the central request of the Aaronic blessing in verses 24 to 26?

options.text: That the LORD bless and keep His people, cause His face to shine on them and be gracious, and lift up His countenance and give them peace.
options.note: This is the blessing's content exactly, in three rising lines.

options.text: That the LORD grant Israel military victory over their enemies.
options.note: Military victory is not the content of this particular blessing.

options.text: That the LORD provide abundant harvests every year.
options.note: Harvests are not mentioned in this blessing.

options.text: The blessing's content is not described.
options.note: Verses 24 to 26 give the blessing's exact wording.

quiz.q: According to verse 27, what is the effect of the priests pronouncing this blessing?

options.text: The LORD puts His own name on the Israelites, and He Himself blesses them.
options.note: Verse 27 states this directly.

options.text: The blessing has only symbolic value with no real effect.
options.note: Verse 27 explicitly states that God Himself blesses as a result.

options.text: The blessing applies only to the priests who speak it.
options.note: The blessing is spoken over the Israelites, not reserved for the priests alone.

options.text: No effect of the blessing is stated.
options.note: Verse 27 states the effect explicitly.

questions: The Nazirite vow was a voluntary way to mark a season of special devotion to God. What would it look like for you to intentionally set apart a season, or even a daily habit, as a visible act of devotion to the Lord?

A single accidental defilement meant the Nazirite's count of days started over completely. Where have you let a single failure convince you that you must abandon a commitment entirely, rather than simply beginning again?

The Aaronic blessing is not a wish but words God commands to be spoken, promising He will actually bless, keep, and give peace to His people. Do you receive God's blessing as something He delights to give, or as something you must earn?

God asks that His face shine upon His people, and that request is answered fully in Jesus, in whom we see the glory of God. Where do you need to remember this week that in Christ, God's face is turned toward you in favor, not judgment?
This blessing closes with God placing His own name on His people. How does knowing you are called by God's name, His own possession, change how you see your identity today?

translationNotes.note: The vow of a Nazirite renders nazir, from a root meaning to separate or consecrate; the term describes the status of the person, not merely the vow itself.
The three-part blessing rises in length and fullness with each line in the Hebrew, a deliberate poetic crescendo often less audible in translation but clear when read aloud in the original.
They shall put My name on the Israelites uses covenant language elsewhere associated with ownership and belonging, so the blessing is inseparable from God claiming His people as His own.
